board.verdict: The through-line advances structurally while the adversary marshals a statistical counter: the BIS 2025 Triennial Survey puts the dollar on one side of 89.2 percent of all FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022. The desk reads that number against the grain. FX transactions are dominated by speculative flows and interbank repo — inertia instruments, not choices renewed. The indicators that track structural shift read differently: dollar reserve share at 58 percent from 72 percent in 2001; China settling roughly half its foreign trade in yuan; mBridge at $55 billion cumulative, 95 percent in digital yuan. The Hormuz yuan toll ended with the Iran deal. The architecture it was a symptom of did not. The AES is now in its third phase — from sovereignty and institutional architecture to economic commons — which is where structural multipolarity becomes material.
board.watching: BRICS Bridge from agenda to adopted framework at the India summit; whether AES mandatory trade roadmap (June 15 ministers' meeting) produces published figures by Q3; whether BRICS Pay has meaningfully reduced USD usage in intra-bloc trade.

sources.label: The Block — mBridge $55B milestone
Asia Times — BRICS dedollarization route

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022 — is the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Currency transaction share is dominated by speculative and hedging flows, not by the reserve accumulation and trade-invoicing trends that determine long-run hegemony. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER.

sources.label: The Block — mBridge, BIS context
Disruption Banking — mBridge

board.verdict: Xi crossed the Yalu and 'denuclearization' did not survive the trip. The Tumen road bridge opens June 19 — two days from writing. No delays reported. Pyongyang will have road access to Russia that bypasses Beijing's customs infrastructure. The desk is watching cargo type and volumes after opening, not the ceremony. Kim's June 9 naval expansion announcement — a second guided missile destroyer, a stated 10,000-ton warship, explicit framing of sea-based nuclear deterrent — represents the next stage: from deterrence in place to projection capability.
board.watching: Cargo volumes and types on the Tumen bridge after June 19 opening; Beijing customs response at the 1959 rail bridge; whether naval expansion (destroyer class, 10,000-ton) moves to keel-laying.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Khasan-Tumangang Bridge
IP Defense Forum — road bridge leverage
AEI Korean Peninsula Update, June 9

board.verdict: The AES is moving through its phases on schedule: sovereignty first, then security architecture, now economic commons. The Alliance's Ministers of Industry and Trade met in Burkina Faso on June 15 and produced a mandate the desk takes seriously: double intraregional trade by 2028, backed by a mandatory roadmap with strict monitoring; domestic processing of gold, uranium, oil, cotton, and livestock rather than export of unrefined goods; a unified internal market. Prime Minister Ouedraogo's language — enough declarations — signals that the political class has noticed the gap between rhetoric and implementation and is closing it. Traoré's gold nationalization (SOPAMIB portfolio, six of fifteen industrial mines) and the UCT-AES territorial commons (established June 11 in Niamey) set the structural conditions; now comes the revenue capture. The desk's watch item: whether the roadmap, when published, carries concrete figures that align with Faso Mêbo construction timelines — that is where the commitment becomes real.
board.watching: Publication of the AES trade roadmap with concrete figures; whether 2028 trade target aligns with Faso Mêbo timelines; gold revenue allocation to the unified force; renegotiated security arrangements.

sources.label: Pravda Burkina Faso — AES trade ministers meeting, June 15

board.verdict: China's May NBS data, released June 16, confirmed the demand-side deterioration the desk was watching. Retail sales fell 0.6% year-on-year — the first decline since December 2022, reversing April's 0.2% gain and missing the Reuters consensus of flat. Fixed-asset investment came in at -4.1% cumulative through January-May, against -1.6% through January-April — the contraction nearly tripling in pace in a single month, against a Reuters consensus of -2.0%. Industrial output held at 4.5% year-on-year, above the 4.3% estimate. The picture is a two-speed economy: production intact, demand deteriorating. The NBS attributed the investment decline to 'high temperatures and heavy rain' and 'the transition from old to new growth drivers.' The desk records this explanation without adopting it. The desk's standing prediction — FAI exits contraction by Q4 2026 — is not falsified (it resolves on January 2027 data), but the May figure requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing, and the remaining quarters give it less room.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS data; whether demand-side stimulus materializes; June retail sales (released mid-July) — see new prediction; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

sources.label: CNBC — China May NBS data, June 16
Reuters/Investing.com — retail sales first decline in three years
CNBC — April NBS data, May 18

board.verdict: The G7 in France on June 16 produced the Atlantic order's first internal public disagreement on the Iran deal: European leaders warned Trump that the framework risks 'entrenching Tehran's nuclear and ballistic missile programs' — an accurate read of the deal's structure, which defers the nuclear chapter to 60-day talks with no enforcement architecture and leaves Iran's 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium without agreed disposition. Netanyahu declared on June 16 that Iran 'will not have nuclear weapons, with or without an agreement' — this is not a diplomatic statement but a declaration of Israeli operational authority independent of Washington's deal. The Atlantic order's claim is that it ended a war. The desk's read: it produced a signing ceremony for Friday and deferred the hardest question, while fracturing along the Iran-nuclear fault line in public, for the first time, at its own summit.
board.watching: G7 communiqué on Ukraine: whether it contains binding aid pledges and delivery timelines or serves as ceremony; whether Netanyahu's Lebanon carve-out destabilizes ceasefire implementation; 60-day nuclear talks architecture.

sources.label: US News — Europeans warn Trump at G7, June 16
Al Jazeera — Iran deal explained, June 15
JNS — Netanyahu: Iran will not have nukes

wars.basis: The desk holds the Russia-decisive call. Overnight June 14-15, Russia launched 70 missiles and 611 drones at Ukraine, with over 60 missiles targeted at Kyiv — 30 casualties, 4 fatalities; the Dormition Cathedral of the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra sustained a roof fire, the Mystetskyi Arsenal was struck. Ukrainian Air Force spokesperson Yurii Ihnat reported 50 missiles and 582 drones neutralized; the desk marks this as a belligerent's account. June 15 saw 192 recorded combat engagements, highest concentration in Pokrovsk. Martyanov's 87% figure (artillery 55%, air 32%) grounds the read: this is fire dominance delivered through modern platforms. The Russia-decisive call holds, with tempo escalating. The desk continues to watch Pokrovsk for confirmed territorial movement.

sources.label: Martyanov (standing reference)
Ukrinform war section — June 15 (Ukrainian source — belligerent's account)
Militarnyi — Rybinsk strategic reserve strike, June 14
PBS NewsHour — record June Russian drone attacks

wars.advantage: Settlement — signing ceremony June 19 in Switzerland; nuclear chapter deferred to 60-day talks
wars.basis: Signing ceremony confirmed for June 19 in Switzerland; Trump declared the deal complete June 15; G7 opened June 16 in France with Zelenskyy present. European G7 leaders publicly warned that the framework risks entrenching Tehran's nuclear and ballistic missile programs — the nuclear chapter remains the unresolved spine: Iran's 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium has no agreed disposition, the full 14-point memorandum has not been published, the 60-day follow-on talks carry no enforcement architecture. Netanyahu declared June 16 that Iran will not have nuclear weapons 'with or without an agreement' — Israeli operational authority declared independent of Washington's framework. The June 14 prediction — formally signed before June 30 — is on track to resolve as a hit in two days. The June 15 prediction — that 60-day talks do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before September 2026 — stands as the desk's live call.

sources.label: US News — Europeans warn Trump at G7, June 16
Al Jazeera — Iran deal questions answered, June 15
JNS — Netanyahu: Iran will not have nukes
CBS News — Iran deal complete, June 15

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Camara killed
PBS NewsHour

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.
